original_abstract:
In the contemporary era, incidents of elder neglect and physical abuse underscore a growing moral crisis regarding the treatment of parents. This study explores the concept of *birr al-wālidayn* (filial piety) through a comparative analysis of Surah al-Isrā’ verses 23-24, drawing upon the classical interpretations of Ibn Kathīr and the contemporary insights of M. Quraish Shihab in *Tafsir al-Mishbah*. Utilizing a qualitative library research methodology, this paper examines how both scholars bridge the theological link between the oneness of God (tauhid) and the mandatory obligation of kindness toward parents.

The findings indicate that both mufassirs view filial piety as an essential individual obligation (*fardhu 'ain*) that demands gentle speech, humility, and constant prayer for one's parents. However, a significant nuanced difference emerges in their interpretation of the term *iḥsān*. Ibn Kathīr defines it broadly as a general command for good conduct and polite interaction. In contrast, Quraish Shihab offers a more comprehensive definition, suggesting that *iḥsān* requires children to bestow favors and perform deeds that exceed the level of kindness they received from their parents. Furthermore, the study highlights Shihab’s linguistic analysis of the preposition "bi," which implies a requirement for a close, intimate, and physical presence in caregiving. Ultimately, the research emphasizes that *birr al-wālidayn* serves as a practical manifestation of faith, requiring a balance of physical care and sincere spiritual devotion regardless of the parents' religious beliefs or circumstances.
humanized_abstract:
The increasing number of cases of elder abuse or neglect in contemporary times is a clear evidence of the ethical decline in the manner in which parents are treated in the society. This paper examines in depth the concept of birr al-wālidayn by referring to Surah al-Isrā’ verses 23-24 in depth by elaborating the tafsir of the said verses by Ibn Kathīr and the contemporary tafsir given by M. Quraish Shihab in Tafsir al-Mishbah. Through qualitative library research, this paper aims to explore how both scholars relate the theistic concept of tauhid with the duty of treating parents with compassion.

According to the research results, both mufassirs have agreed on that looking after parents is one of the individual obligations (*fardhu ‘ain*) that requires courteous words and humble and obedient deed. In addition, both mufassirs also agreed on that term iḥsān has been explained by Ibn Kathīr as an invitation to behave morally and speak kindly. In his turn, Quraish Shihab defined it as the children rendering the parents greater services and goodness than what the parents had rendered to them. Another important point that the research discovered was that both mufassirs explained the preposition “bi” in the phrase (بيقا بأسede ب) as a particle that requires mutual contact with closeness, relation, or even physical approach as long as it is for the sake of serving and caring for the parents. Thus, looking after one’s parents is considered by both mufassirs as a means to exemplify the Islamic belief as it is supposed to embody both physical and spiritual dimensions in which the children provide what their parents need under all circumstances whether the parents were Muslims or not, or whether they enjoyed all the requirements of life, or were deprived of them.
